From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Nickname for members of an athletic team

|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | **This article has multiple issues.** Please help **[improve it](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Athletic_nickname "Special:EditPage/Athletic nickname")** or discuss these issues on the **[talk page](/wiki/Talk:Athletic_nickname "Talk:Athletic nickname")**. *([Learn how and when to remove these messages](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |  |  | | --- | --- | | [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Athletic_nickname "Special:EditPage/Athletic nickname") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Athletic nickname"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Athletic+nickname%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Athletic+nickname%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Athletic+nickname%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Athletic+nickname%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Athletic+nickname%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Athletic+nickname%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(July 2007)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |  |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | This article **may contain [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Athletic_nickname&action=edit) by [verifying](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") the claims made and adding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources"). Statements consisting only of original research should be removed. *(October 2014)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |  *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The **athletic nickname**, or equivalently **athletic moniker**, of a [university or college within the United States](/wiki/Higher_education_in_the_United_States "Higher education in the United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Higher_education_in_Canada "Higher education in Canada") is the name officially adopted by that institution for at least the members of its [athletic](/wiki/Sport "Sport") [teams](/wiki/Team "Team"). Typically as a matter of engendering [school spirit](/wiki/School_spirit "School spirit"), the institution either officially or unofficially uses this [moniker](/wiki/Moniker "Moniker") of the institution's athletic teams also as a [nickname](/wiki/Nickname "Nickname") to refer to people associated with the institution, especially its current [students](/wiki/Student "Student"), but also often its [alumni](/wiki/Alumni "Alumni"), its [faculty](/wiki/Academic_staff "Academic staff"), and its [administration](/wiki/Academic_administration "Academic administration") as well. This practice at the university and college tertiary higher-education level has proven so popular that it extended to the high school secondary-education level in the United States and Canada and even to the primary-education level as well.

## Themes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Athletic_nickname&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Themes")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **may contain [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Athletic_nickname&action=edit) by [verifying](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") the claims made and adding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources"). Statements consisting only of original research should be removed. *(April 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In the United States and Canada, multiple recurring themes have appeared over time for choosing a school's athletic nickname. In almost all cases, the institution chooses an athletic nickname with an overtly positive goal in mind, where that goal reflects the character of the institution—either a previously established characteristic or a characteristic hoped for as a goal henceforth.

### Abstract concept

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Athletic_nickname&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Abstract concept")]

Often by choosing an abstract concept as its athletic moniker, the institution wants to inspire its student-athletes on and off the field to achieve success that the abstract concept represents. Examples: [Cornell Big Red](/wiki/Cornell_Big_Red "Cornell Big Red"), [Stanford Cardinal](/wiki/Stanford_Cardinal "Stanford Cardinal"), [UIC Flames](/wiki/UIC_Flames "UIC Flames"), [Tulane Green Wave](/wiki/Tulane_Green_Wave "Tulane Green Wave").

### Animal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Athletic_nickname&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Animal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/2018-0716-Big12MD-Cy.jpg/250px-2018-0716-Big12MD-Cy.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018-0716-Big12MD-Cy.jpg)

[Cy the Cardinal](/wiki/Cy_the_Cardinal "Cy the Cardinal"), mascot of [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University")

Often by choosing an [animal](/wiki/Animal "Animal"), the school wants to emphasize the instillation of [fear](/wiki/Fear "Fear") of losing athletic competitions to the institution's teams, such as through an especially fierce or stealthy animal. When the school chooses an animal as its athletic nickname, usually in the plural or as a collective noun for a group of that animal, then typically, the school has that animal (in the singular) as its [mascot](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot"),[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) either specifically named with a proper noun or generically referred to without a proper noun. Examples: [Michigan Wolverines](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines "Michigan Wolverines"), [Oregon Ducks](/wiki/Oregon_Ducks "Oregon Ducks"), [Princeton Tigers](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers "Princeton Tigers"), [Iowa Hawkeyes](/wiki/Iowa_Hawkeyes "Iowa Hawkeyes"), [California Golden Bears](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears "California Golden Bears"), [Minnesota Golden Gophers](/wiki/Minnesota_Golden_Gophers "Minnesota Golden Gophers"), [Texas Longhorns](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns "Texas Longhorns") and the [Bowling Green Falcons](/wiki/Bowling_Green_Falcons "Bowling Green Falcons").

### Collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Athletic_nickname&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Collection")]

Often by choosing a collection that represents a summary of the institution's students or of its history. Such a collection may refer to an [ethnicity](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity"); a [profession](/wiki/Profession "Profession"); religious designation, such as saints; or other groupings of people. A portion of athletic monikers that fall into this collection category started originally as [derogatory](/wiki/Derogatory "Derogatory") [epithets](/wiki/Epithet "Epithet") from others, but as an act of defiance, the school embraced the term as a rallying cry to overcome the term's negative origin. Because a collection is hard to represent or [iconify](/wiki/Icon "Icon"), when a school chooses a collection as its athletic nickname, the school typically chooses a related but different [mascot](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot") that symbolizes that collection. Examples: [Notre Dame Fighting Irish](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish "Notre Dame Fighting Irish"), [Oklahoma Sooners](/wiki/Oklahoma_Sooners "Oklahoma Sooners"), [Purdue Boilermakers](/wiki/Purdue_Boilermakers "Purdue Boilermakers"), [Illinois Fighting Illini](/wiki/Illinois_Fighting_Illini "Illinois Fighting Illini"), [Texas A&M Aggies](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_Aggies "Texas A&M Aggies")

### Hero or archetype

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Athletic_nickname&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Hero or archetype")]

A small number of schools choose an [archetypical](/wiki/Archetype "Archetype") heroic person as their official athletic nickname. This person may be a graduate of the school who is viewed as embodying the school's mission or an archetypal person who is symbolic of the school's area, such as the [West Virginia University Mountaineer](/wiki/West_Virginia_Mountaineer "West Virginia Mountaineer"). In religiously affiliated schools, this person may be an historical person who has received an official designation in that religion, such as a [saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint") in the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") or [Eastern Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church").

### Native American likeness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Athletic_nickname&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Native American likeness")]

Main article: [Native American mascot controversy](/wiki/Native_American_mascot_controversy "Native American mascot controversy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Atlanta_Braves_fan_with_tomahawk.jpg/250px-Atlanta_Braves_fan_with_tomahawk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlanta_Braves_fan_with_tomahawk.jpg)

The [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves") encouraged fans to gesture with the [tomahawk chop](/wiki/Tomahawk_chop "Tomahawk chop"), distributing foam [tomahawks](/wiki/Tomahawk "Tomahawk") at games and other events.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Likenesses to [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") were at one time widely popular athletic monikers, especially for schools that adopted them in the 19th or early 20th century. In recent years, some Native American organizations have protested the unlicensed use of likenesses of Native Americans related to team names, team [logos](/wiki/Logo "Logo"), athletic monikers, [cheerleaders](/wiki/Cheerleader "Cheerleader"), and cheering techniques. The granting of overtly expressed written licenses by Native American organizations to use likenesses of Native Americans in these ways is rare, although not unheard of. In one notable example, two major groups of the [Seminole](/wiki/Seminole "Seminole") nation, the Seminole Tribe of Florida and Seminole Nation of Oklahoma, have expressly given [Florida State University](/wiki/Florida_State_University "Florida State University") permission to use the nickname "Seminoles" and certain Seminole imagery. [Central Michigan University](/wiki/Central_Michigan_University "Central Michigan University") has a similar arrangement with the [Saginaw Chippewa Indian Tribe](/wiki/Saginaw_Chippewa_Indian_Tribe "Saginaw Chippewa Indian Tribe") to use the name "Chippewas".

Because of protests from some Native American organizations, some schools have changed their athletic moniker and mascot and cheering practices without significant objection once the issue was raised, especially if such offense toward a group of people was viewed as incompatible with that school's stated mission or if the threat of legal action was too burdensome. Other schools or their student bodies have defended their use of Native American likenesses, especially if the institution views the use of Native American likenesses as respectful or so intimately tied with history to be inseparable from the institution, such as if the name of institution derives from the name of a tribe. Still other schools have embarked on a series of failed attempts to find a replacement.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

### Common and uncommon names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Athletic_nickname&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Common and uncommon names")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Fixed_Ramblin%27_Wreck.jpg/250px-Fixed_Ramblin%27_Wreck.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fixed_Ramblin%27_Wreck.jpg)

The official mascot of the [Georgia Institute of Technology](/wiki/Georgia_Institute_of_Technology "Georgia Institute of Technology") is the [Ramblin' Wreck](/wiki/Ramblin%27_Wreck "Ramblin' Wreck"), a 1930 [Ford Model A](/wiki/Ford_Model_A_(1927) "Ford Model A (1927)") [sport coupe](/wiki/Sport_coupe "Sport coupe").

Often, certain nicknames (animals and some abstract concepts, such as [Giants](/wiki/Giant "Giant"), [Broncos](/wiki/Bronco "Bronco"), or [Wildcats](/wiki/Wildcat "Wildcat")) have become very common. However, some nicknames are unique to that school/team such as [Illini](/wiki/Illinois_Fighting_Illini "Illinois Fighting Illini"), [Demon Deacons](/wiki/Wake_Forest_Demon_Deacons "Wake Forest Demon Deacons") or [Fords](/wiki/Haverford_Fords "Haverford Fords").

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Athletic_nickname&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Lists of nicknames](/wiki/Lists_of_nicknames "Lists of nicknames") – nickname list articles on Wikipedia

2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Anderson, L. V. (2012-09-26). ["Where Did the Tomahawk Chop Come From?"](https://slate.com/news-and-politics/2012/09/origins-of-the-tomahawk-chop-scott-browns-staffers-mocking-elizabeth-warren-are-continuing-a-long-tradition.html). *Slate Magazine*. Retrieved 2019-10-10.
